title: II
html:
II

She found an early opportunity to carry on her investigations. For, at dinner that night, she found herself, Tietjens having gone to the telephone with a lance-corporal, opposite what she took to be a small tradesman, with fresh-coloured cheeks, and a great, grey, forward-sprouting moustache, in a uniform so creased that the creases resembled the veins of a leafпїњвАКпїњвА¶ A very trustworthy small tradesman: the grocer from round the corner whom, sometimes, you allow to supply you with paraffinпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He was saying to her:

вАЬIf, maвАЩam, you multiply two-thousand nine hundred and something by ten you arrive at twenty-nine thousand oddпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

And she had exclaimed:

вАЬYou really mean that my husband, Captain Tietjens, spent yesterday afternoon in examining twenty-nine thousand toenailsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And two thousand nine hundred toothbrushesпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

вАЬI told him,вАЭ her interlocutor answered with deep seriousness, вАЬthat these being Colonial troops it was not so necessary to examine their toothbrushesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Imperial troops will use the brush they clean their buttons with for their teeth so as to have a clean toothbrush to show the medical officerпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

вАЬIt sounds,вАЭ she said with a little shudder, вАЬas if you were all schoolboys playing a gameпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And you say my husband really occupies his mind with such thingsпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Second-Lieutenant Cowley, dreadfully conscious that the shoulder-strap of his Sam Browne belt, purchased that afternoon at the Ordnance, and therefore brand-new, did not match the abdominal part of the belt that he had had for nearly ten yearsпїњвАФa splendid bit of leather, that!пїњвАФanswered nevertheless stoutly:

вАЬMadam! If the brains of an army arenвАЩt, the life of an army isпїњвАКпїњвА¶ in its feetпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And nowadays, the medical officers say, in its teethпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Your husband, maвАЩam, is an admirable officerпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He says that no draft he turns out shallпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She said:

вАЬHe spent three hours inпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You say, foot and kit inspectionпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Second-Lieutenant Cowley said:

вАЬOf course he had other officers to help him with the kitпїњвАКпїњвА¶ but he looked at every foot himselfпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She said:

вАЬThat took him from two till fiveпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Then he had tea, I supposeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And went toпїњвАКпїњвА¶ What is it?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ The papers of the draftпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Second-Lieutenant Cowley said, muffled through his moustache:

вАЬIf the captain is a little remiss in writing lettersпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I have heardпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You might, madamпїњвАКпїњвА¶ IвАЩm a married man myselfпїњвАКпїњвА¶ with a daughterпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And the army is not very good at writing lettersпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You might say, in that respect, that thank God we have got a navy, maвАЩamпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She let him stagger on for a sentence or two, imagining that, in his confusion, she might come upon traces of Miss Wannop in Rouen. Then she said handsomely:

вАЬOf course you have explained everything, Mr.¬†Cowley, and I am very much obligedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Of course my husband would not have time to write very full lettersпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He is not like the giddy young subalterns who run afterпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

He exclaimed in a great roar of laughter:

вАЬThe captain run after skirtsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Why, I can number on my hands the times heвАЩs been out of my sight since heвАЩs had the battalion!вАЭ

A deep wave of depression went over Sylvia.

вАЬWhy,вАЭ Lieutenant Cowley laughed on, вАЬif we had a laugh against him it was that he mothered the lot of us as if he was a hen sitting on addled eggsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For itвАЩs only a ragtime army, as the saying is, when youвАЩve said the best for it that you canпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And look at the other commanding officers weвАЩve had before we had himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ There was Major BrooksпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Never up before noon, if then, and out of camp by two-thirty. Get your returns ready for signing before then or never get вАЩem signedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And Colonel PotterпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Bless my soulпїњвАКпїњвА¶ вАЩe wouldnвАЩt sign any blessed papers at allпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He lived down here in this hotel, and we never saw him up at the camp at allпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But the captainпїњвАКпїњвА¶ We always say thatпїњвАКпїњвА¶ if вАЩe was a Chelsea adjutant getting off a draft of the Second ColdstreamsпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

With her indolent and gracious beautyпїњвАФSylvia knew that she was displaying indolent and gracious beautyпїњвАФSylvia leaned over the tablecloth listening for items in the terrible indictment that, presently, she was going to bring against TietjensпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For the morality of these matters is this:пїњвАКпїњвА¶ If you have an incomparably beautiful woman on your hands you must occupy yourself solely with herпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Nature exacts that of youпїњвАКпїњвА¶ until you are unfaithful to her with a snubnosed girl with freckles: that, of course, being a reaction, is still in a way occupying yourself with your woman!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ But to betray her with a battalionпїњвАКпїњвА¶ That is against decency, against NatureпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And for him, Christopher Tietjens, to come down to the level of the men you met here!пїњвАКпїњвА¶

Tietjens, mooning down the room between tables, had more than his usually aloof air since he had just come out of a telephone box. He slipped, a weary mass, into the polished chair between her and the lieutenant. He said:

вАЬIвАЩve got the washing arranged forпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ and Sylvia gave to herself a little hiss between the teeth, of vindictive pleasure! This was indeed betrayal to a battalion. He added: вАЬI shall have to be up in camp before four-thirty tomorrow morningпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Sylvia could not resist saying:

вАЬIsnвАЩt there a poemпїњвАКпїњвА¶ вАШAh me, the dawn, the dawn, it comes too soon!вАЩпїњвАКпїњвА¶ said of course by lovers in bed?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Who was the poet?вАЭ

Cowley went visibly red to the roots of his hair and evidently beyond. Tietjens finished his speech to Cowley, who had remonstrated against his going up to the camp so early by saying that he had not been able to get hold of an officer to march the draft. He then said in his leisurely way:

вАЬThere were a great many poems with that refrain in the Middle AgesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You are probably thinking of an albade by Arnaut Daniel, which someone translated latelyпїњвАКпїњвА¶ An albade was a song to be sung at dawn when, presumably, no one but lovers would be likely to singпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

вАЬWill there,вАЭ Sylvia asked, вАЬbe anyone but you singing up in your camp tomorrow at four?вАЭ

She could not help itпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She knew that Tietjens had adopted his slow pomposity in order to give the grotesque object at the table with them time to recover from his confusion. She hated him for it. What right had he to make himself appear a pompous ass in order to shield the confusion of anybody?

The second-lieutenant came out of his confusion to exclaim, actually slapping his thigh:

вАЬThere you are, madamпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Trust the captain to know everything!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ I donвАЩt believe thereвАЩs a question under the sun you could ask him that he couldnвАЩt answerпїњвАКпїњвА¶ They say up at the campпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ He went on with long stories of all the questions Tietjens had answered up at the campпїњвАКпїњвА¶

Emotion was going all over SylviaпїњвАКпїњвА¶ at the proximity of Tietjens. She said to herself: вАЬIs this to go on forever?вАЭ Her hands were ice-cold. She touched the back of her left hand with the fingers of her right. It was ice-cold. She looked at her hands. They were bloodlessпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She said to herself: вАЬItвАЩs pure sexual passionпїњвАКпїњвА¶ itвАЩs pure sexual passionпїњвАКпїњвА¶ God! CanвАЩt I get over this?вАЭ She said: вАЬFather!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ You used to be fond of ChristopherпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Get our Lady to get me over thisпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ItвАЩs the ruin of him and the ruin of me. But, oh damn, donвАЩt!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ For itвАЩs all I have to live forпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ She said: вАЬWhen he came mooning back from the telephone I thought it was all rightпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I thought what a heavy wooden-horse he lookedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For two minutesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Then itвАЩs all over me againпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I want to swallow my saliva and I canвАЩt. My throat wonвАЩt workпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She leaned one of her white bare arms on the tablecloth towards the walrus-moustache that was still snuffling gloriously:

вАЬThey used to call him Old Sol at school,вАЭ she said. вАЬBut thereвАЩs one question of SolomonвАЩs he could not answerпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The one about the way of a man withпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Oh, a maid!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Ask him what happened before the dawn ninety-sixпїњвАФno, ninety-eight days agoпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She said to herself: вАЬI canвАЩt help itпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Oh, I canвАЩt help itпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

The ex-sergeant-major was exclaiming happily:

вАЬOh, no one ever said the captain was one of these thought-readersпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ItвАЩs real solid knowledge of men and things he hasпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Wonderful how he knows the men considering he was not born in the serviceпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But there, your born gentleman mixes with men all his days and knows them. Down to the ground and inside their putteesпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Tietjens was looking straight in front of him, his face perfectly expressionless.

вАЬBut I bet I got himпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ she said to herself and then to the sergeant-major:

вАЬI suppose now an army officerпїњвАФone of your born gentlemenпїњвАФwhen a back-from-leave train goes out from any of the great stationsпїњвАФPaddington, sayпїњвАФto the frontпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He knows how all the men are feelingпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But not what the married women thinkпїњвАКпїњвА¶ or theпїњвАКпїњвА¶ the girlпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She said to herself: вАЬDamn it, how clumsy I am getting!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ I used to be able to take his hide off with a word. Now I take sentences at a timeпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She went on with her uninterrupted sentence to Cowley: вАЬOf course he may never be going to see his only son again, so it makes him sensitiveпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The officer at Paddington, I meanпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She said to herself: вАЬBy God, if that beast does not give in to me tonight he never shall see Michael againпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Ah, but I got himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Tietjens had his eyes closed, round each of his high-coloured nostrils a crescent of whiteness was beginning. And increasingпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She felt a sudden alarm and held the edge of the table with her extended arm to steady herselfпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Men went white at the nose like that when they were going to faintпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She did not want him to faintпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But he had noticed the word PaddingtonпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Ninety-eight days beforeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She had counted every day sinceпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She had got that much informationпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She had said Paddington outside the house at dawn and he had taken it as a farewell. He hadпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He had imagined himself free to do what he liked with the girlпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Well, he wasnвАЩtпїњвАКпїњвА¶ That was why he was white about the gillsпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Cowley exclaimed loudly:

вАЬPaddington!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ It isnвАЩt from there that back-from-leave trains go. Not for the front: the B.E.F.пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Not from PaddingtonпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The Glamorganshires go from there to the depotпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And the LiverpoolsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ TheyвАЩve got a depot at BirkenheadпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Or is that the Cheshires?пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ He asked of Tietjens: вАЬIs it the Liverpools or the Cheshires that have a depot at Birkenhead, sir?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ You remember we recruited a draft from there when we were at PenhallyпїњвАКпїњвА¶ At any rate, you go to Birkenhead from PaddingtonпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I was never there myselfпїњвАКпїњвА¶ They say itвАЩs a nice placeпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Sylvia saidпїњвАФshe did not want to say it:

вАЬItвАЩs quite a nice placeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ but I should not think of staying there foreverпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Tietjens said:

вАЬThe Cheshires have a training campпїњвАФnot a depotпїњвАФnear Birkenhead. And of course there are R.G.A.s thereпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ She had been looking away from himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Cowley exclaimed:

вАЬYou were nearly off, sir,вАЭ hilariously. вАЬYou had your peepers shutпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ Lifting a champagne glass, he inclined himself towards her. вАЬYou must excuse the captain, maвАЩam,вАЭ he said. вАЬHe had no sleep last nightпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Largely owing to my faultпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Which is what makes it so kind of himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I tell you, maвАЩam, there are few things I would not do for the captainпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ He drank his champagne and began an explanation: вАЬYou may not know, maвАЩam, this is a great day for meпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And you and the captain are making it the greatest day of my lifeпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ Why, at four this morning there hadnвАЩt been a wretcheder man in Ruin townпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And nowпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He must tell her that he suffered from an unfortunateпїњвАФa miserableпїњвАФcomplaintпїњвАКпїњвА¶ One that makes one have to be careful of celebrationsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And today was a day that he had to celebrateпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But he dare not have done it where Sergeant-Major Ledoux is along with a lot of their old matesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ вАЬI dare notпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I dussnвАЩt!вАЭ he finishedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ вАЬSo I might have been sitting, now, at this very moment, up in the cold campпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But for you and the captainпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Up in the cold campпїњвАКпїњвА¶ YouвАЩll excuse me, maвАЩamпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Sylvia felt that her lids were suddenly wavering:

вАЬI might have been myself,вАЭ she said, вАЬin a cold camp, tooпїњвАКпїњвА¶ if I hadnвАЩt thrown myself on the captainвАЩs mercy!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ At Birkenhead, you knowпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I happened to be there till three weeks agoпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ItвАЩs strange that you mentioned itпїњвАКпїњвА¶ There are things like signsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ but youвАЩre not a Catholic! They could hardly be coincidencesпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She was tremblingпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She looked, fumblingly opening it, into the little mirror of her powder-boxпїњвАФof chased, very thin gold with a small blue stone, like a forget-me-not in the centre of the concentric engravingsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ DrakeпїњвАФthe possible father of MichaelпїњвАФhad given it to herпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The first thing he had ever given her. She had brought it down tonight out of defiance. She imagined that Tietjens disliked itпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She said breathlessly to herself: вАЬperhaps the damn thing is an ill omenпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ Drake had been the first man who had everпїњвАКпїњвА¶ A hot-breathed brute!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ In the little glass her features were chalk-whiteпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She looked likeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ she looked likeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She had a dress of golden tissueпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The breath was short between her white set teethпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Her face was as white as her teethпїњвАКпїњвА¶ AndпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Yes! Nearly! Her lipsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ What was her face like?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ In the chapel of the convent of Birkenhead there was a tomb all of alabasterпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She said to herself:

вАЬHe was near faintingпїњвАКпїњвА¶ IвАЩm near faintingпїњвАКпїњвА¶ WhatвАЩs this beastly thing thatвАЩs between us?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ If I let myself faintпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But it would not make that beastвАЩs face any less wooden!пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She leaned across the table and patted the ex-sergeantmajorвАЩs black-haired hand:

вАЬIвАЩm sure,вАЭ she said, вАЬyouвАЩre a very good manпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ She did not try to keep the tears out of her eyes, remembering his words: вАЬUp in the cold camp.вАЭпїњвАКпїњвА¶ вАЬIвАЩm glad the captain, as you call him, did not leave you in the cold campпїњвАКпїњвА¶ YouвАЩre devoted to him, arenвАЩt you?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ There are others he does leaveпїњвАКпїњвА¶ up inпїњвАКпїњвА¶ the cold campпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For punishment, you knowпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

The ex-sergeant-major, the tears in his eyes too, said:

вАЬWell, there is men you вАЩas to give the C.B. toпїњвАКпїњвА¶ C.B. means confined to barracksпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

вАЬOh, there are!вАЭ she exclaimed. вАЬThere are!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ And women, tooпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Surely there are women, too?пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

The sergeant-major said:

вАЬWacks, perвАЩapsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I donвАЩt knowпїњвАКпїњвА¶ They say womenвАЩs discipline is like oursпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Founded on hours!вАЭ

She said:

вАЬDo you know what they used to say of the captain?пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ She said to herself: вАЬI pray to God the stiff, fatuous beast likes sitting here listening to this stuffпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Blessed Virgin, mother of God, make him take meпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Before midnight. Before elevenпїњвАКпїњвА¶ As soon as we get rid of thisпїњвАКпїњвА¶ No, heвАЩs a decent little manпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Blessed Virgin!вАЭпїњвАКпїњвА¶ вАЬDo you know what they used to say of the captain?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ I heard the warmest banker in England say it of himпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

The sergeant-major, his eyes enormously opened, said:

вАЬDid you know the warmest banker in England?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ But there, we always knew the captain was well connectedпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ She went on:

вАЬThey said of himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He was always helping people.вАЭпїњвАКпїњвА¶ вАЬHoly Mary, mother of God!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ HeвАЩs my husbandпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ItвАЩs not a sinпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Before midnightпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Oh, give me a signпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Or beforeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ the termination of hostilitiesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ If you give me a sign I could wait.вАЭпїњвАКпїњвА¶ вАЬHe helped virtuous Scotch students, and broken-down gentryпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And women taken in adulteryпїњвАКпїњвА¶ All of themпїњвАКпїњвА¶ LikeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You know WhoпїњвАКпїњвА¶ That is his modelпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ She said to herself: вАЬCurse him!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ I hope he likes itпїњвАКпїњвА¶ YouвАЩd think the only thing he thinks about is the beastly duck heвАЩs wolfing down.вАЭпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And then aloud: вАЬThey used to say: вАШHe saved others; himself he could not saveпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЩвАКвАЭ

The ex-sergeant-major looked at her gravely:

вАЬMaвАЩam,вАЭ he said, вАЬwe couldnвАЩt say exactly that of the captainпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For I fancy it was said of our RedeemerпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But we вАЩave said that if ever there was a poor bloke the captain could вАЩelp, вАЩelp вАЩim вАЩe wouldпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Yet the unit was always getting вАЩellish strafe from headquartersпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Suddenly Sylvia began to laughпїњвАКпїњвА¶ As she began to laugh she had rememberedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The alabaster image in the nunвАЩs chapel at Birkenhead the vision of which had just presented itself to her, had been the recumbent tomb of an honourable Mrs.¬†Tremayne-WarlockпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She was said to have sinned in her youthпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And her husband had never forgiven herпїњвАКпїњвА¶ That was what the nuns saidпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She said aloud:

It antagonized her, and it was a weight upon her, for hitherto she had completely ignored them: in this place they seemed to have a coherence, a massпїњвАКпїњвА¶ almost a lifeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ They rushed in and out of rooms occupied, as incomprehensibly, as unpresentably, with things like boots, washing, vaccination certificatesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Even with old tins!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ A man with prematurely white hair and a pasty face, with a tunic that bulged both above and below his belt, would walk into the drawing-room of a lady who superintended all the acid-drop and cigarette stalls of that city and remark to a thin-haired, deaf man with an amazingly red noseпїњвАФa nose that had a perfectly definite purple and scarlet diagonal demarcation running from the bridge to the upper side of the nostrilsпїњвАФthat he had got his old tins off his hands at last. He would have to repeat it in a shout because the red-nosed man, his head hanging down, would have heard nothing at all. The deaf man would say Humph! Humph! Snuffle. The woman giving the teaпїњвАФa Mrs.¬†Hemmerdine, of Tarbolton, whom you might have met at home, would be saying that at last she had got twelve reams of notepaper with forget-me-nots in the top corners when the deaf-faced man would begin, gruffly and uninterruptedly, a monologue on his urgent need for twenty thousand tons of sawdust for the new slow-burning stoves in the menвАЩs hutsпїњвАКпїњвА¶

It was undeniably like something movingпїњвАКпїњвА¶ All these things going in one directionпїњвАКпїњвА¶ A disagreeable force set in motion by gawky schoolboysпїњвАФbut schoolboys of the Sixth Form, sinister, hobbledehoy, waiting in the corners of playgrounds to torture someone, weak and unfortunateпїњвАКпїњвА¶ In one or other corner of their worldwide playground they had come upon Father Consett and hanged him. No doubt they tortured him first. And, if he made an offering of his sufferings, then and there to Heaven, no doubt he was already in paradiseпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Or, if he was not yet in heaven, certain of these souls in purgatory were yet listened to in the midst of their tormentsпїњвАКпїњвА¶

So she said:

вАЬBlessed and martyred father, I know that you loved Christopher and wish to save him from trouble. I will make this pact with you. Since I have been in this room I have kept my eyes in the boatпїњвАФalmost in my lap. I will agree to leave off torturing Christopher and I will go into retreat in a convent of Ursuline Dames NoblesпїњвАФfor I canвАЩt stand the nuns of that other conventпїњвАФfor the rest of my lifeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And I know that will please you, too, for you were always anxious for the good of my soulпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ She was going to do that if when she raised her eyes and really looked round the room she saw in it one man that looked presentable. She did not ask that he should more than look presentable, for she wanted nothing to do with the creature. He was to be a sign: not a prey!

She explained to the dead priest that she could not go all the world over to see if it contained a presentable man, but she could not bear to be in a convent forever, and have the thought that there wasnвАЩt, for other women, one presentable man in the worldпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For Christopher would be no good to them. He would be mooning forever over the Wannop girl. Or her memory. That was all oneпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He was content with loveпїњвАКпїњвА¶ If he knew that the Wannop girl was loving him in Bedford Park, and he in the Khyber States with the Himalayas between them, he would be quite contentпїњвАКпїњвА¶ That would be correct in its way, but not very helpful for other womenпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Besides, if he were the only presentable man in the world, half the women would be in love with himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And that would be disastrous, because he was no more responsive than a bullock in a fatting pen.

вАЬSo, father,вАЭ she said, вАЬwork a miracleпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ItвАЩs not very much of a little miracleпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Even if a presentable man doesnвАЩt exist you could put him thereпїњвАКпїњвА¶ IвАЩll give you ten minutes before I lookпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She thought it was pretty sporting of her, for, she said to herself, she was perfectly in earnest. If in that long, dim, green-lamp-shaded, and of course be-palm-leaved, badly-proportioned, glazed, ignoble public room, there appeared one decentish man, as decentish men went before this beanfeast began, she would go into retreat for the rest of her lifeпїњвАКпїњвА¶

She fell into a sort of dim trance after she had looked at her watch. Often she went into these dim trancesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ever since she had been a girl at school with Father Consett for her spiritual adviser!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ She seemed to be aware of the father moving about the room, lifting up a book and putting it downпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Her ghostly friend!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Goodness, he was unpresentable enough, with his broad, open face that always looked dirtyish, his great dark eyes, and his great mouthпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But a saint and a martyrпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She felt him thereпїњвАКпїњвА¶ What had they murdered him for? Hanged at the word of a half-mad, half-drunk subaltern, because he had heard the confession of some of the rebels the night before they were takenпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He was over in the far corner of the roomпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She heard him say: they had not understood, the men that had hanged him. That is what you would say, fatherпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Have mercy on them, for they know not what they doпїњвАКпїњвА¶

Then have mercy on me, for half the time I donвАЩt know what IвАЩm doing!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ It was like a spell you put on me. At Lobscheid. Where my mother was, when I came back from that place without my clothesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You said, didnвАЩt you, to mother, but she told me afterwards: The real hell for that poor boy, meaning Christopher, will come when he falls in love with some young girlпїњвАФas, mark me, he willпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For she, meaning me, will tear the world down to get at himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And when mother said she was certain I would never do anything vulgar you obstinately did not agreeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You knew meпїњвАКпїњвА¶

She tried to rouse herself and said: He knew meпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Damn it he knew me!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ WhatвАЩs vulgarity to me, Sylvia Tietjens, born Satterthwaite? I do what I want and thatвАЩs good enough for anyone. Except a priest. Vulgarity! I wonder mother could be so obtuse. If I am vulgar IвАЩm vulgar with a purpose. Then itвАЩs not vulgarity. It may be vice. Or viciousnessпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But if you commit a mortal sin with your eyes open itвАЩs not vulgarityпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You chance hell fire foreverпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Good enough!

The weariness sank over her again and the sense of the fatherвАЩs presenceпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She was back again in Lobscheid, thirty-six hours free of Perowne with the father and her mother in the dim sitting-room, all antlers, candle-lit, with the fatherвАЩs shadow waving over the pitch-pine walls and ceilingsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ It was a bewitched place, in the deep forest of Germany. The father himself said it was the last place in Europe to be Christianized. Or perhaps it was never ChristianizedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ That was perhaps why those people, the Germans, coming from those deep, devil-infested woods, did all these wickednesses. Or maybe they were not wickedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ One would never know properlyпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But maybe the father had put a spell on herпїњвАКпїњвА¶ His words had never been out of her mind, muchпїњвАКпїњвА¶ At the back of her brain, as the saying wasпїњвАКпїњвА¶

Some man drifted near her and said:

вАЬHow do you do, Mrs.¬†Tietjens? Who would have thought of seeing you here?вАЭ

She answered:

вАЬI have to look after Christopher now and then.вАЭ He remained hanging over her with a schoolboy grin for a minute, then he drifted away as an object sinks into deep waterпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Father Consett again hovered near her. She exclaimed:

вАЬBut the real point is, fatherпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Is it sporting?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Sporting or whatever it is?вАЭ And Father Consett breathed: вАЬAh!пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ with his terrible power of arousing doubtsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She said:

вАЬWhen I saw ChristopherпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Last night?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Yes, it was last nightпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Turning back to go up that hillпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And I had been talking about him to a lot of grinning private soldiersпїњвАКпїњвА¶ To madden himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You mustnвАЩt make scenes before the servantsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ A heavy man, tiredпїњвАКпїњвА¶ come down the hill and lumbering up againпїњвАКпїњвА¶ There was a searchlight turned on him just as he turnedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I remembered the white bulldog I thrashed on the night before it diedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ A tired, silent beastпїњвАКпїњвА¶ with a fat white behindпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Tired outпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You couldnвАЩt see its tail because it was turned down, the stumpпїњвАКпїњвА¶ A great, silent beastпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The vet said it had been poisoned with red lead by burglarsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ItвАЩs beastly to die of red leadпїњвАКпїњвА¶ It eats up the liverпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And you think youвАЩre getting better for a fortnight. And youвАЩre always coldпїњвАКпїњвА¶ freezing in the blood-vesselsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And the poor beast had left its kennel to try and be let in to the fireпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And I found it at the door when I came in from a dance without ChristopherпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And got the rhinoceros whip and lashed into itпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ThereвАЩs a pleasure in lashing into a naked white beastпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Obese and silentпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Like ChristopherпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I thought Christopher mightпїњвАКпїњвА¶ That nightпїњвАКпїњвА¶ It went through my headпїњвАКпїњвА¶ It hung down its headпїњвАКпїњвА¶ A great head, room for a whole British encyclopaedia of misinformation, as Christopher used to put itпїњвАКпїњвА¶ It said: вАШWhat a hope!вАЩпїњвАКпїњвА¶ As I hope to be saved, though I never shall be, the dog said: вАШWhat a hope!вАЩпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Snow-white in quite black bushesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And it went under a bushпїњвАКпїњвА¶ They found it dead there in the morningпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You canвАЩt imagine what it looked like, with its head over its shoulder, as it looked back and said: вАШWhat a hopeвАЩ to meпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Under a dark bush. An euпїњвАКпїњвА¶ euпїњвАКпїњвА¶ euonymus, isnвАЩt it?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ In thirty degrees of frost with all the blood-vessels exposed on the naked surface of the skinпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ItвАЩs the seventh circle of hell, isnвАЩt it? the frozen oneпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The last stud-white bulldog of that breedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ As Christopher is the last stud-white hope of the Groby Tory breedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Modelling himself on our LordпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But our Lord was never married. He never touched on topics of sex. Good for HimпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She said: вАЬThe ten minutes is up, fatherпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ and looked at the round, starred surface between the diamonds of her wrist watch. She said: вАЬGood God!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Only one minuteпїњвАКпїњвА¶ IвАЩve thought all that in only one minuteпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I understand how hell can be an eternityпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Christopher, very weary, and ex-Sergeant-Major Cowley, very talkative by now, loomed down between palms. Cowley was saying: вАЬItвАЩs infamous!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ ItвАЩs past bearingпїњвАКпїњвА¶ To reorder the draft at elevenпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ They sank into chairsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Sylvia extended towards Tietjens a small packet of letters. She said: вАЬYou had better look at theseпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I had your letters sent to me from the flat as there was so much uncertainty about your movementsпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ She found that she did not dare, under Father ConsettвАЩs eyes, to look at Tietjens as she said that. She said to Cowley: вАЬWe might be quiet for a minute or two while the captain reads his lettersпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Have another liqueur?пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She then observed that Tietjens just bent open the top of the letters from Mrs.¬†Wannop and then opened that from his brother Mark.

вАЬCurse it,вАЭ she said, вАЬIвАЩve given him what he wants!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ He knowsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ HeвАЩs seen the addressпїњвАКпїњвА¶ that theyвАЩre still in Bedford ParkпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He can think of the Wannop girl as thereпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He has not been able to know, till now, where she isпїњвАКпїњвА¶ HeвАЩll be imagining himself in bed with her thereпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Father Consett, his broad, unmodelled dark face full of intelligence and with the blissful unction of the saint and martyr, was leaning over TietjensвАЩ shoulderпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He must be breathing down ChristopherвАЩs back as, her mother said, he always did when she held a hand at auction and he could not play because it was between midnight and his celebrating the holy massпїњвАКпїњвА¶

She said:

вАЬNo, I am not going madпїњвАКпїњвА¶ This is an effect of fatigue on the optic nervesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Christopher has explained that to meпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He says that when his eyes have been very tired with making one of his senior wranglerвАЩs calculations he has often seen a woman in an eighteenth-century dress looking into a drawer in his bureauпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Thank God, IвАЩve had Christopher to explain things to meпїњвАКпїњвА¶ IвАЩll never let him goпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Never, never, let him goпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

It was not, however, until several hours later that the significance of the fatherвАЩs apparition came to her and those intervening hours were extraordinarily occupiedпїњвАФwith emotions, and even with action. To begin with, before he had read the fewest possible words of his brotherвАЩs letter, Tietjens looked up over it and said:

вАЬOf course you will occupy GrobyпїњвАКпїњвА¶ With MichaelпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Naturally the proper business arrangements will be madeпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ He went on reading the letter, sunk in his chair under the green shade of a lampпїњвАКпїњвА¶

The letter, Sylvia knew, began with the words: вАЬYour вАФпїњвАФпїњвАФ of a wife has been to see me with the idea of getting any allowance I might be minded to make you transferred to herself. Of course she can have Groby, for I shanвАЩt let it, and could not be bothered with it myself. On the other hand, you may want to live at Groby with that girl and chance the racket. I should if I were you. You would probably find the place worth theпїњвАКпїњвА¶ what is it? ostracism, if there was anyпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But IвАЩm forgetting that the girl is not your mistress unless anything has happened since I saw youпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And you probably would want Michael to be brought up at Groby, in which case you couldnвАЩt keep the girl there, even if you camouflaged her as governess. Of course if you want your wife to have Groby she must have enough to run it with credit, and expenses are rising damnably. Still, our incomings rise not a little, too, which is not the case with some. The only thing I insist on is that you make plain to that baggage that whatever I allow her, even if itвАЩs no end of a hot income, not one penny of it comes out of what I wish you would allow me to allow you. I mean I want you to make plain to that rouged pieceпїњвАФor perhaps itвАЩs really natural, my eyes are not what they wereпїњвАФthat what you have is absolutely independent of what she sucks up as the mother of our fatherвАЩs heir and to keep our fatherвАЩs heir in the state of life that is his dueпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I hope you feel satisfied that the boy is your son, for itвАЩs more than I should be, looking at the partyпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But even if he is not he is our fatherвАЩs heir all right and must be so treatedпїњвАКпїњвА¶

вАЬBut be plain about that, for the trollop came to me, if you please, with the proposal that I should dock you of any income I might propose to allow youпїњвАФand to which of course you are absolutely entitled under our fatherвАЩs will, though it is no good reminding you of that!пїњвАФas a token from me that I disapproved of your behaviour when, damn it, there is not an action of yours that I would not be proud to have to my credit. At any rate in this affair, for I cannot help thinking that you could be of more service to the country if you were anywhere else but where you are. But you know what your conscience demands of you better than I, and I dare say these hellcats have so mauled you that you are glad to be able to get away into any hole. I did not tell the discreditable daughter that sheпїњвАФher motherпїњвАФhad come to see me at breakfast immediately after seeing you off, she was so upset. And she keawert ho down iвАЩ thвАЩ ingle and had a gradely pow. You remember how Gobbles the gardener used to say that. A good chap, though he came from Lancasheere!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ The mother has no illusions about the daughter and is heart and soul for you. She was dreadfully upset at your going, the more so as she believes that itвАЩs her offspring has driven you out of the country and that you purposeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ isnвАЩt stopping one the phrase? DonвАЩt do that.

вАЬI saw your girl yesterdayпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She looked peaky. But of course I have seen her several times, and she always looks peaky. I do not understand why you do not write to them. The mother is clamorous because you have not answered several letters and have not sent her military information she wants for some article she is writing for a Swiss magazineпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Sylvia knew the letter almost by heart as far as that because in the unbearable white room of the convent near Birkenhead she had twice begun to copy it out, with the idea of keeping the copies for use in some sort of publicity. But, at that point, she had twice been overcome by the idea that it was not a very sporting thing to do, if you really think about it. Mark, in his naive way, was concerned that the old lady, although now enjoying the income from the legacy left her by their father, had not immediately settled down to write a deathless novel; although, as he added, he knew nothing about novelsпїњвАКпїњвА¶

Christopher was reading away at his letters beneath the green-shaded lamp; the ex-quartermaster had begun several sentences and dropped into demonstrative silence at the reminder that Tietjens was reading. ChristopherвАЩs face was completely without expression; he might have been reading a return from the office of statistics in the old days at breakfast. She wondered, vaguely, if he would see fit to apologize for the epithets that his brother had applied to her. Probably he would not. Accompanying ChristopherвАЩs mind, as it were, whilst he read his letter had induced in her a rather different mood. The pictures in her own mind were rather of MarkвАЩs dingy breakfast-room in which she had had her interview with himпїњвАФand of the outside of the dingy house in which the Wannops lived, at Bedford ParkпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But she was still conscious of her pact with the father and, looking at her wrist watch, saw that by now six minutes had passedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ It was astonishing that Mark, who was a millionaire at least, and probably a good deal more, should live in such a dingy apartmentпїњвАФit had for its chief decoration the hoofs of several deceased race-winners, mounted as inkstands, as pen-racks, as paperweightsпїњвАФand afford himself only such a lugubrious breakfast of fat slabs of ham over which bled pallid eggsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For she too, like her mother, had looked in on Mark at breakfast-timeпїњвАФher mother because she had just seen Christopher off to France, and she because, after a sleepless nightпїњвАФthe third of a seriesпїњвАФshe had been walking about St.¬†JamesвАЩs Park and, passing under MarkвАЩs windows, it had occurred to her that she might do Christopher some damage by putting his brother wise about the entanglement with Miss Wannop. So, on the spur of the moment, she had invented a desire to live at Groby with the accompanying necessity for additional means. For, although she was a pretty wealthy woman, she was not wealthy enough to live at Groby and keep it up. The immense old place was not so immense because of its room-space, though, as far as she could remember, there must be anything between forty and sixty rooms, but because of the vast old grounds, the warren of stabling, wells, rose-walks and fencingпїњвАКпїњвА¶ A manвАЩs place, really, the furniture very grim and the corridors on the ground floor all slabbed with great stones. So she had looked in on Mark, reading his correspondence with his copy of The Times airing on a chair-back before the fireпїњвАФfor he was just the man to retain the eighteen-forty idea that you catch cold by reading a damp newspaper. His grim, tight, brown-wooden features that might have been carved out of an old chair, had expressed no emotion at all during the interview. He had offered to have up some more ham and eggs for her and had asked one or two questions as to how she meant to live at Groby if she went there. Otherwise he had said nothing about the information she had given him as to the Wannop girl having had a baby by ChristopherпїњвАФfor purposes of conversation she had adhered to that old story, at any rate till that interview. He had said nothing at all. Not one wordпїњвАКпїњвА¶ At the end of the interview, when he had risen and produced from an adjoining room a bowler hat and an umbrella, saying that he must now go to his office, he had put to her without any expression pretty well what stood in the letter, as far as business was concerned. He said that she could have Groby, but she must understand that, his father being now dead and he a public official, without children and occupied in London with work that suited him, Groby was practically ChristopherвАЩs property to do what he liked with as long asпїњвАФwhich he certainly wouldпїњвАФhe kept it in proper style. So that, if she wished to live there, she must produce ChristopherвАЩs authorization to that effect. And he added, with an equableness so masking the proposition that it was not until she was well out of the house and down the street that its true amazingness took her breath away:

вАЬOf course, Christopher, if what you say is true, might want to live at Groby with Miss Wannop. In that case he would have to.вАЭ And he had offered her an expressionless hand and shepherded her, rather fussily, through his dingy and awkward front passages that were lit only from ground-glass windows giving apparently on to his bathroomпїњвАКпїњвА¶

It wasnвАЩt until that moment, really, that, at once with exhilaration and also with a sinking at the heart, she realized what she was up against in the way of a combination. For, when she had gone to MarkвАЩs, she had been more than half-maddened by the news that Christopher at Rouen was in hospital and, although the hospital authorities had assured her, at first by telegram and then by letter, that it was nothing more than his chest, she had not had any knowledge of to what extent Red Cross authorities did or did not mislead the relatives of casualties.

So it had seemed natural that she should want to inflict on him all the injuries that she could at the moment, the thought that he was probably in pain making her wish to add all she could to that painпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Otherwise, of course, she would not have gone to MarkвАЩsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For it was a mistake in strategy. But then she said to herself: вАЬConfound it!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ What strategy was it a mistake in? What do I care about strategy? What am I out for?пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ She did what she wanted to, on the spur of the moment!пїњвАКпїњвА¶

Now she certainly realized. How Christopher had got round Mark she did not know or much care, but there Christopher certainly was, although his father had certainly died of a broken heart at the rumours that were going round about his sonпїњвАФrumours she, almost as efficiently as the man called Ruggles and more irresponsible gossips, had set going about Christopher. They had been meant to smash Christopher: they had smashed his father insteadпїњвАКпїњвА¶

But Christopher had got round Mark, whom he had not seen for ten yearsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Well, he probably would. Christopher was perfectly immaculate, that was a fact, and Mark, though he appeared half-witted in a North Country way, was no fool. He could not be a fool. He was a really august public official. And, although as a rule Sylvia gave nothing at all for any public official, if a man like Mark had the position by birth amongst presentable men that he certainly ought to have and was also the head of a department and reputed absolutely indispensableпїњвАФyou could not ignore himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He said, indeed, in the later, more gossipy parts of his letter that he had been offered a baronetcy, but he wanted Christopher to agree with his refusing it. Christopher would not want the beastly title after his death, and for himself he would be rather struck with the pip than let that harlotпїњвАФmeaning herselfпїњвАФbecome Lady T. by any means of his. He had added, with his queer solicitude, вАЬOf course if you thought of divorcingпїњвАФwhich I wish to God you would, though I agree that you are right not toпїњвАФand the title would go to the girl after my decease IвАЩd take it gladly, for a title is a bit of a help after a divorce. But as it is I propose to refuse it and ask for a knighthood, if it wonвАЩt too sicken you to have me a SirпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For I hold no man ought to refuse an honour in times like these, as has been done by certain sickening intellectuals, because it is like slapping the sovereign in the face and bound to hearten the other side, which no doubt was what was meant by those fellows.вАЭ

There was no doubt that MarkпїњвАФwith the possible addition of the WannopsпїњвАФmade a very strong backing for Christopher if she decided to make a public scandal about himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ As for the WannopsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ the girl was negligible. Or possibly not, if she turned nasty and twisted Christopher round her fingers. But the old mother was a formidable figureпїњвАФwith a bad tongue, and viewed with a certain respect in places where people talkedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ both on account of her late husbandвАЩs position and of the solid sort of articles she wroteпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She, Sylvia, had gone to take a look at the place where these people livedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ a dreary street in an outer suburb, the housesпїњвАФshe knew enough about estates to knowпїњвАФwhat is called вАЬtile-healed,вАЭ the upper parts of tile, the lower flimsy brick and the tiles in bad condition. Oldish houses really, in spite of their sham artistic aspect, and very much shadowed by old trees that must have been left to add to the picturesquenessпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The rooms poky, and they must be very darkпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The residence of extreme indigence, or of absolute povertyпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She understood that the old ladyвАЩs income had so fallen off during the war that they had nothing to live on but what the girl made as a schoolteacher, or a teacher of athletics in a girlsвАЩ schoolпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She had walked two or three times up and down the street with the idea that the girl might come out: then it had struck her that that was rather an ignoble proceeding, reallyпїњвАКпїњвА¶ It was, for the matter of that, ignoble that she should have a rival who starved in an ashbinпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But that was what men were like: she might think herself lucky that the girl did not inhabit a sweetshopпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And the man, Macmaster, said that the girl had a good head and talked well, though the woman Macmaster said that she was a shallow ignoramusпїњвАКпїњвА¶ That last was probably not true; at any rate the girl had been the Macmaster womanвАЩs most intimate friend for many yearsпїњвАФas long as they were sponging on Christopher and until, lower middle-class snobs as they were, they began to think that they could get into Society by carneying to herselfпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Still, the girl probably was a good talker and, if little, yet physically uncommonly fitпїњвАКпїњвА¶ A good homespun articleпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She wished her no ill!

What was incredible was that Christopher should let her go on starving in such a poverty-stricken place when he had something like the wealth of the Indies at his disposalпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But the Tietjens were hard people! You could see that in MarkвАЩs roomsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ and Christopher would lie on the floor as lief as in a goose-feather bed. And probably the girl would not take his money. She was quite right. That was the way to keep himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ She herself had no want of comprehension of the stimulation to be got out of parsimonious livingпїњвАКпїњвА¶ In retreat at her convent she lay as hard and as cold as any anchorite, and rose to the nunsвАЩ matins at four.

It was not, in fact, their fittings or food that she objected toпїњвАФit was that the lay-sisters, and some of the nuns, were altogether too much of the lower classes for her to like to have always about herпїњвАКпїњвА¶ That was why it was to the Dames Nobles that she would go, if she had to go into retreat for the rest of her life, according to contractпїњвАКпїњвА¶

A gun manned by exhilarated antiaircraft fellows, and so close that it must have been in the hotel garden, shook her physically at almost the same moment as an immense maroon popped off on the quay at the bottom of the street in which the hotel was. She was filled with annoyance at these schoolboy exercises. A tall, purple-faced, white-moustached general of the more odious type, appeared in the doorway and said that all the lights but two must be extinguished and, if they took his advice, they would go somewhere else. There were good cellars in the hotel. He loafed about the room extinguishing the lights, couples and groups passing him on the way to the doorпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Tietjens looked up from his letterпїњвАФhe was now reading one of Mrs.¬†WannopвАЩsпїњвАФbut seeing that Sylvia made no motion he remained sunk in his chairпїњвАКпїњвА¶

The old general said:

вАЬDonвАЩt get up, TietjensпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Sit down, lieutenantпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Mrs.¬†Tietjens, I presumeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But of course I know you are Mrs.¬†TietjensпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ThereвАЩs a portrait of you in this weekвАЩsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I forget the nameпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ He sat down on the arm of a great leather chair and told her of all the trouble her escapade to that city had caused himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He had been awakened immediately after a good lunch by some young officer on his staff who was scared to death by her having arrived without papers. His digestion had been deranged ever sinceпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Sylvia said she was very sorry. He should drink hot water and no alcohol with lunch. She had had very important business to discuss with Tietjens, and she had really not understood that they wanted papers of grownup people. The general began to expatiate on the importance of his office and the number of enemy agents his perspicacity caused to be arrested every day in that city and the lines of communicationпїњвАКпїњвА¶

Sylvia was overwhelmed at the ingenuity of Father Consett. She looked at her watch. The ten minutes were up, but there did not appear to be a soul in the dim placeпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The father hadпїњвАФand no doubt as a Sign that there could be no mistaking!пїњвАФcompletely emptied that room. It was like his humour!

To make certain, she stood up. At the far end of the room, in the dimness of the one other reading lamp that the general had not extinguished, two figures were rather indistinguishable. She walked towards them, the general at her side extending civilities all over her. He said that she need not be under any apprehension there. He adopted that device of clearing the room in order to get rid of the beastly young subalterns who would use the place to spoon in when the lights were turned down. She said she was only going to get a timetable from the far end of the roomпїњвАКпїњвА¶

The stab of hope that she had that one of the two figures would turn out to be the presentable man diedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ They were a young mournful subaltern, with an incipient moustache and practically tears in his eyes, and an elderly, violently indignant baldheaded man in evening civilian clothes that must have been made by a country tailor. The general said that it was one of the young cubs on his own staff getting a dressing down from his dad for spending too much money. The young devils would get amongst the girlsпїњвАФand the old ones too. There was no stopping it. The place was a hotbed ofпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He left the sentence unfinished. She would not believe the trouble it gave himпїњвАКпїњвА¶ That hotel itselfпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The scandalsпїњвАКпїњвА¶

He said she would excuse him if he took a little nap in one of the armchairs too far away to interfere with their business talk. He would have to be up half the night. He seemed to Sylvia a blazingly contemptible personageпїњвАФtoo contemptible really for Father Consett to employ as an agent, in clearing the roomпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But the omen was given. She had to consider her position. It meantпїњвАФor did it?пїњвАФthat she had to be at war with the heavenly powers!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ She clenched her handsпїњвАКпїњвА¶

In passing by Tietjens in his chair the general boomed out the words:

вАЬI got your chit of this morning, Tietjens I must sayпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Tietjens lumbered out of his chair and stood at attention, his leg-of-mutton hands stiffly on the seams of his breeches.

вАЬItвАЩs pretty strong,вАЭ the general said, вАЬmarking a charge-sheet sent down from my department: Case explained. We donвАЩt lay charges without due thought. And Lance-Corporal Berry is a particularly reliable N.C.O. I have difficulty enough to get them. Particularly after the late riots. It takes courage, I can tell you.вАЭ

вАЬIf,вАЭ Tietjens said, вАЬyou would see fit, sir, to instruct the G.M.P. not to call Colonial troops вАШdamned conscripts,вАЩ the trouble would be overпїњвАКпїњвА¶ WeвАЩre instructed to use special discretion, as officers, in dealing with troops from the Dominions. They are said to be very susceptible of insultпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

The general suddenly became a boiling pot from which fragments of sentences came away: damned insolence; court of inquiry; damned conscripts they were too. He calmed enough to say:

вАЬThey are conscripts, your men, arenвАЩt they? They give me more troubleпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I should have thought that you would have wantedпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Tietjens said:

вАЬNo, sir. I have not a man in my unit, as far as itвАЩs Canadian or British Columbian, that is not voluntarily enlistedпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

The general exploded to the effect that he was bringing the whole matter before the G.O.C.I.C.вАЩs department. Campion could deal with it how he wished: it was beyond himself. It was difficult for Sylvia to get hold again of her thoughts in the smoking-room, for the evening was entirely pervaded with military effects that seemed to her the pranks of schoolboys. Indeed, after Cowley, who had by now quite a good skinful of liquor, had said to Tietjens:

вАЬBy Jove, I would not like to be you and a little bit on if old Blazes caught sight of you tonight,вАЭ she said to Tietjens with real wonder:

Tietjens said:

вАЬWell, itвАЩs a troublesome business, all thisпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

She said that it so appeared to be, for before he could finish his sentence an orderly was at his elbow extending, along with a pencil, a number of dilapidated papers. Tietjens looked rapidly through them, signing one after the other and saying intermittently:

вАЬItвАЩs a trying time.вАЭ вАЬWeвАЩre massing troops up the line as fast as we can go.вАЭ вАЬAnd with an endlessly changing personnelпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ He gave a snort of exasperation and said to Cowley: вАЬThat horrible little Pitkins has got a job as bombing instructor. He canвАЩt march the draftпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Who the deuce am I to detail? Who the deuce is there?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ You know all the littleпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ He stopped because the orderly could hear. A smart boy. Almost the only smart boy left him.

Cowley barged out of his seat and said he would telephone the mess to see who was thereпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Tietjens said to the boy:

вАЬSergeant-Major Morgan made out these returns of religions in the draft?вАЭ

The boy answered: вАЬNo, sir, I did. TheyвАЩre all right.вАЭ He pulled a slip of paper out of his tunic pocket and said shyly:

вАЬIf you would not mind signing this, sirпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I can get a lift on an A.S.C. trolley thatвАЩs going to Boulogne tomorrow at sixпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

Tietjens said:

вАЬNo, you canвАЩt have leave. I canвАЩt spare you. WhatвАЩs it for?вАЭ

The boy said almost inaudibly that he wanted to get married.

Tietjens, still signing, said: вАЬDonвАЩtпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Ask your married pals what itвАЩs like!вАЭ

The boy, scarlet in his khaki, rubbed the sole of one foot on the instep of the other. He said that saving madamвАЩs presence it was urgent. It was expected any day now. She was a real good gel. Tietjens signed the boyвАЩs slip and handed it to him without looking up. The boy stood with his eyes on the ground. A diversion came from the telephone, which was at the far end of the room. Cowley had not been able to get on to the camp because an urgent message with regard to German espionage was coming through to the sleeping general.

Cowley began to shout: вАЬFor goodnessвАЩ sake hold the lineпїњвАКпїњвА¶ For goodnessвАЩ sake hold the lineпїњвАКпїњвА¶ IвАЩm not the generalпїњвАКпїњвА¶ IвАЩm not the generalпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ Tietjens told the orderly to awaken the sleeping warrior. A violent scene at the mouth of the quiescent instrument took place. The general roared to know who was the officer speakingпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Captain BubbleyjocksпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Captain CuddlestocksпїњвАКпїњвА¶ what in hellвАЩs name! And who was he speaking for?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Who? Himself?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Urgent was it?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ DidnвАЩt he know the proper procedure was by writing?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Urgent damnation!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Did he not know where he was?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ In the First Army by the Cassell CanalпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Well thenпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But the spy was in L. of C. territory, across the canalпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The French civilian authorities were very concernedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ They were, damn them!пїњвАКпїњвА¶ And damn the officer. And damn the French maire. And damn the horse the supposed spy rode uponпїњвАКпїњвА¶ And when the officer was damned let him write to First Army Headquarters about it and attach the horse and the bandoliers as an exhibitпїњвАКпїњвА¶

There was a great deal more of it. Tietjens, reading his papers still, intermittently explained the story as it came in fragments over the telephone in the generalвАЩs repetitionsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Apparently the French civilian authorities of a place called Warendonck had been alarmed by a solitary horseman in English uniform who had been wandering desultorily about their neighbourhood for several days, seeming to want to cross the canal bridges, but finding them guardedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ There was an immense artillery dump in the neighbourhood, said to be the largest in the world, and the Germans dropped bombs as thick as peas all over those parts in the hopes of hitting itпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Apparently the officer speaking was in charge of the canal bridgehead guards; but, as he was in First Army country, it was obviously an act of the utmost impropriety to awaken a general in charge of the spy-catching apparatus on the other side of the canalпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The general, returning past them to an armchair farther from the telephone, emphasized this point of view with great vigour.

The orderly had returned; Cowley went once more to the telephone, having consumed another liqueur brandy. Tietjens finished his papers and went through them rapidly again. He said to the boy: вАЬGot anything saved up?вАЭ The boy said: вАЬA fiver and a few bob.вАЭ Tietjens said: вАЬHow many bob?вАЭ The boy: вАЬSeven, sir.вАЭ Tietjens, fumbling clumsily in an inner pocket and a little pocket beneath his belt, held out one leg-of-mutton fist and said: вАЬThere! That will double it. Ten pounds fourteen! But itвАЩs very improvident of you. See that you save up a deuced lot more against the next one. Accouchements are confoundedly expensive things, as youвАЩll learn, and ring money doesnвАЩt stretch forever!пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ He called out to the retreating boy: вАЬHere, orderly, come backпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ He added: вАЬDonвАЩt let it get all over campпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I canвАЩt afford to subsidize all the seven-months children in the battalionпїњвАКпїњвА¶ IвАЩll recommend you for paid lance-corporal when you return from leave if you go on as well as you have done.вАЭ He called the boy back again to ask him why Captain McKechnie had not signed the papers. The boy stuttered and stammered that Captain McKechnie wasпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He wasпїњвАКпїњвА¶

Tietjens muttered: вАЬGood God!вАЭ beneath his breath. He said:

вАЬThe captain has had another nervous breakdownпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ The orderly accepted the phrase with gratitude. That was it. A nervous breakdown. They say he had been very queer at mess. About divorce. Or the captainвАЩs uncle. A barrow-night! Tietjens said: вАЬYes, yes.вАЭ He half rose in his chair and looked at Sylvia. She exclaimed painfully:

вАЬYou canвАЩt go. I insist that you canвАЩt go.вАЭ He sank down again and muttered wearily that it was very worrying. He had been put in charge of this officer by General Campion. He ought not to have left the camp at all perhaps. But McKechnie had seemed better. A great deal of the calmness of her insolence had left her. She had expected to have the whole night in which luxuriously to torment the lump opposite her. To torment and to allure him. She said:

вАЬYou have settlements to come to now and here that will affect your whole life. Our whole lives! You propose to abandon them because a miserable little nephew of your miserable little friendпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ She added in French: вАЬEven as it is you cannot pay attention to these serious matters, because of these childish preoccupations of yours. That is to be intolerably insulting to me!вАЭ She was breathless.

Tietjens asked the orderly where Captain McKechnie was now. The orderly said he had left the camp. The colonel of the depot had sent a couple of officers as a search-party. Tietjens told the orderly to go and find a taxi. He could have a ride himself up to camp. The orderly said taxis would not be running on account of the air-raid. Could he order the G.M.P. to requisition one on urgent military service? The exhilarated airgun pooped off thereupon three times from the garden. For the next hour it sent off every two or three minutes. Tietjens said: вАЬYes! Yes!вАЭ to the orderly. The noises of the air raid became more formidable. A blue express letter of French civilian make was handed to Tietjens. It was from the duchess to inform him that coal for the use of greenhouses was forbidden by the French Government. She did not need to say that she relied on his honour to ensure her receiving her coal through the British military authorities, and she asked for an immediate reply. Tietjens expressed real annoyance while he read this. Distracted by the noise, Sylvia cried out that the letter must be from Valentine Wannop in Rouen. Did not the girl intend to let him have an hour in which to settle the whole business of his life? Tietjens moved to the chair next to hers. He handed her the duchessвАЩs letter.

He began a long, slow, serious explanation with a long, slow, serious apology. He said he regretted very much that when she should have taken the trouble to come so far in order to do him the honour to consult him about a matter which she would have been perfectly at liberty to settle for herself, the extremely serious military position should render him so liable to interruption. As far as he was concerned Groby was entirely at her disposal with all that it contained. And of course a sufficient income for the upkeep.

She exclaimed in an access of sudden and complete despair:

вАЬThat means that you do not intend to live there.вАЭ He said that that must settle itself later. The war would no doubt last a good deal longer. While it lasted there could be no question of his coming back. She said that that meant that he intended to get killed. She warned him that, if he got killed, she would cut down the great cedar at the southwest corner of Groby. It kept all the light out of the principal drawing-room and the bedrooms above itпїњвАКпїњвА¶ He winced: he certainly winced at that. She regretted that she had said it. It was along other lines that she desired to make him wince.

He said that, apart from his having no intention of getting himself killed, the matter was absolutely out of his hands. He had to go where he was ordered to go and do what he was told to do.

She exclaimed:

вАЬYou! You! IsnвАЩt it ignoble. That you should be at the beck and call of these ignoramuses. You!вАЭ

He went on explaining seriously that he was in no great dangerпїњвАФin no danger at all unless he was sent back to his battalion. And he was not likely to be sent back to his battalion unless he disgraced himself or showed himself negligent where he was. That was unlikely. Besides his category was so low that he was not eligible for his battalion, which, of course, was in the line. She ought to understand that everyone that she saw employed there was physically unfit for the line. She said:

вАЬThatвАЩs why theyвАЩre such an awful lotпїњвАКпїњвА¶ It is not to this place that one should come to look for a presentable manпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Diogenes with his lantern was nothing to it.вАЭ

He said:

вАЬThereвАЩs that way of looking at itпїњвАКпїњвА¶ It is quite true that most ofпїњвАКпїњвА¶ letвАЩs say your friendsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ were killed off during the early days, or if theyвАЩre still going theyвАЩre in more active employments.вАЭ What she called presentableness was very largely a matter of physical fitnessпїњвАКпїњвА¶ The horse, for instance, that he rode was rather a crockпїњвАКпїњвА¶ But though it was German and not thoroughbred it contrived to be up to his weightпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Her friends, more or less, of before the war were professional soldiers or of the type.
